Plastic Pollution and its Economic Drivers

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source
Author

Jonathan Flores Martinez, Avery Stewart, Justin Mai

The Context of our Data

Original Data Set

This was a data frame with information on plastic pollution. It was originally collected by the Break Free From Plastic (BFFP) group. They are an environmental activist with an interest in reducing the amount of plastic waste in the world. They focus on reducing the use of single use plastics and other actions across the entire life cycle of plastics. They believe that the best way to address plastic pollution comes from prevention over treating the effects of pollution. The data is two years (2019 and 2020) worth of data collected through global audits that BFFP does trying to understand the sources of plastic waste creation. The information provided by the data set is the country, parent company which created the plastic waste, the number of clean up events and volunteers, and the counts of plastic waste. This was unit-less so we are assuming individual plastic pieces collected at the event. There was a grand total count and multiple columns representing the different types of plastics ranging from the thin material of bags to PVC pipe plastic. Each row represents a country and company combination. There are over 10,000 rows of data.

This data was originally found in multiple folders one each for the years 2019 and 2020. Each folder then had a separate csv file for each country. jonthegeek used map_dfr to combine all the csv files into a single data frame. This function also changed the data types. All were turned into doubles as they were numbers except for the country and parent company as these were characters. He then added a year column as the ideas was to eventually combine the two years together. After cleaning up the names with janitor he did the same thing to the 2020 data. This one had some duplicate columns so he used an if_else function to take care of that. Finally the two years were bound together and some names were changed to make it easier to work with and we now have the data set we will be using.

Country Statistics Data Set

The second dataset was obtained through a country statistics API and provide demographic and economic indicators for the countries represented in the plastics data. In this dataset, the observational unit is the country. Variables retrieved from the API include measures such as GDP, population, population density, employment by sector, and other indicators. The documentation for the API does not clearly identify the exact reference year for every variable, so this analysis uses variables that are relatively stable across the 2019–2020 period. The data is important since it provides demographic and economic context for understanding country differences in plastic collected during cleanup audits.

Country Environmental Data Set

The third dataset was obtained through the Global Footprint Network API by pulling country level data for 2019 and 2020 from the National Footprint and Biocapacity Accounts. The API returns environmental and development indicators for countries and territories, including metrics such as Total Biocapacity and Total Ecological Footprint. After retrieving the data, I combined the yearly responses, reshaped them into a country year format, and matched them to the plastics dataset using country and year. This allowed me to incorporate environmental context into the original dataset and compare plastic collection outcomes with a nations ecological state.

Research Questions

Plastic pollution is not only an environmental problem, but also a social and economic one. Different countries consume, regulate, and dispose of plastic in different ways, so cleanup audits can reveal both what kinds of plastics are most common and how broader characteristics of countries may shape pollution patterns. Given this, we wanted to pose two research questions that guide our analysis first.

Question 1

Which plastic types are the most abundant in the audits, does this change from country to country?

Question 2

How do demographic and economic characteristics of countries along with their geographic characteristics associated with differences in plastic collected during cleanup audits?

Research Question 1: Plastic Composition Across Countries

Tables

To address the first research question, we begin by comparing the composition of plastic waste across countries and years. The following tables summarize the proportions and overall audit profiles for the five most populous countries in the dataset. These summaries help identify which plastic categories are most common and whether those patterns appear consistent across countries.

Table 1. Proportions of Plastics for the 5 Most Populated Countries

Show the code 
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(httr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
library(ggridges)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(viridis)
library(ggtext)
library(broom)
library(scales)
library(car)

plastics <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2021/2021-01-26/plastics.csv')

plastics_clean <- plastics |> 
  mutate(across(empty:pvc, ~replace_na(., 0)),
         total = rowSums(across(c(empty:pvc))),
         .before = num_events)
final_merged <- readRDS("final_merged.rds")
#final_merged <- read_csv("/Users/avery/Desktop/stat541/project-1/final_table.csv")
Show the code 
proportions <- function(df) {
  
  df |>
    select(country:pvc) |>
    map_if(is.numeric, ~.x / df$grand_total) |>
    as.data.frame() 
}

plastics_clean |>
    mutate(year = factor(year)) |>
    group_by(country, year) |>
    summarize(across(empty:total, 
                     ~sum(.x, na.rm = TRUE)),
              .groups = "drop") |>
  proportions() |>
  filter(country %in% c("India",
                        "China",
                        "United States of America",
                        "Indonesia",
                        "Nigeria")) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_percent(columns = empty:pvc) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()) |>
  tab_caption("Percentage of Each Plastic Type Found in Audits From 
              the 5 Most Populated Countries by Year") |>
  cols_move_to_end(o) |>
  cols_label(country = "Country", 
             year = "Year",
             empty = "Empty",
             hdpe = "HDPE",
             ldpe = "LDPE",
             o = "Other",
             pet = "PET",
             pp = "PP",
             ps = "PS",
             pvc = "PVC")  |>
  data_color(
    method = "numeric",
    palette = "plasma", 
    reverse = FALSE
  )

Table 2. Summary of Audits for the 10 Countries with the Highest Amount of Plastic Collected

Show the code 
country_profile <- function(data, group_var1, group_var2, min_audits = 10) {
  data |>
    filter(!is.na(country), country != "EMPTY") |>
    group_by({{ group_var1 }}, {{ group_var2 }}) |>
    summarise(
      audits = n(),
      total_pieces = sum(grand_total),
      median_pieces = median(grand_total),
      mean_pieces = mean(grand_total),
      max_pieces = max(grand_total),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) |>
    filter(audits >= min_audits) |>
    arrange({{ group_var1 }}, {{ group_var2 }}) |>
    mutate(country = str_to_title(country)) |>
    arrange(desc(total_pieces)) |>
    slice_head(n = 10) |>
    mutate(country = str_remove(country, "_")) |>
    gt() |> 
    tab_header(
      title = md(
        paste0(
          "**Table.** Plastic cleanup profile by country and year for countries 
          with at least ",
           min_audits,
          " audits."
        )
      )
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(audits, total_pieces, median_pieces, mean_pieces, max_pieces),
    decimals = 1,
    use_seps = TRUE
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    country = md("**Country**"),
    year = md("**Year**"),
    audits = md("**Audits**"),
    total_pieces = md("**Total Pieces**"),
    median_pieces = md("**Median Pieces**"),
    mean_pieces = md("**Mean Pieces**"),
    max_pieces = md("**Max Pieces**")
  )
}


country_profile(plastics_clean, country, year, min_audits = 1)

Visualizations

Figure 1. Lollipop Plot of Average Plastic Waster per Audit for 2019 and 2020 for the 5 Most Populated Countries

Show the code 
rate_ify <- function(df, numerator, denominator, name = rate){
  stopifnot(is.data.frame(df))
  df |>
    mutate({{name}} := {{numerator}}/{{denominator}})
}

plastics_clean |> 
  group_by(country, year) |> 
  summarise(total_trash = sum(total),
            num_events = mean(num_events),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  rate_ify(numerator = total_trash,
           denominator = num_events,
           name = avg_event_impact) |> 
  mutate(country = str_to_title(country)) |>
  filter(country %in% c("India",
                        "China",
                        "United States Of America",
                        "Indonesia", 
                        "Nigeria")) |>
  mutate(country = fct_reorder(country, avg_event_impact)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = avg_event_impact, y = country)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = avg_event_impact,
                   y = country, yend = country),
               color = "gray60") +
  geom_point(size = 4, color = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~ year) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Waste Accounted for per Audit",
    subtitle = "Top 5 Most Populated Countries",
    x = "Average Waste per Audit",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_event_impact, 1)), 
          hjust = -0.3, 
          size = 3) +
  theme_minimal()

Research Question 2: Economic and Geographic Patterns in Plastic Collection

Visualizations

The following visualizations display how plastic waste patterns differ across broader economic and geographic groupings.

Figure 1. Ridge Plot Showing Plastic Waste Type Distribution by Country GDP per Capita

Show the code 
final_merged |> 
  filter(year == 2019) |> 
  group_by(country) |> 
  summarize(unknown = sum(empty),
            hdpe = sum(hdpe),
            ldpe = sum(ldpe),
            other = sum(o),
            pet = sum(pet),
            pp = sum(pp),
            ps = sum(ps),
            pvc = sum(pvc),
            total = sum(total),
            gdp_per_capita = mean(gdp_per_capita),
            hdi = mean(HDI),
            .groups = "drop") |> 
  mutate(income_group = case_when(
    gdp_per_capita < 1500 ~ "Low Income",
    gdp_per_capita > 12000 ~ "High Income",
    TRUE ~ "Middle Income")) |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = unknown:pvc,
    names_to = "plastic_type",
    values_to = "waste") |> 
  filter(waste <= 3500) |> 
  
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = waste,
                       y = plastic_type,
                       fill = income_group)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_viridis(discrete=TRUE) +
  labs(
    title = "Plastic Waste Type Distribution of Countries by GDP per Capita",
    subtitle = "Divided into <span style='color: #6a546f;'>High Income</span>, 
      <span style='color: #b5aa5f;'>Middle Income</span>,
    and <span style='color: #5e8684;'>Low Income</span>",
    x = "Waste Amount",
    y = "",
    alt = "Density ridge plot showing the density of plastic waste audited
    for multiple types of plastic. Colored by GDP per capita category,
    High, Middle, and Low") +
  theme_dark() +
  theme(plot.subtitle =  element_markdown(),
        legend.position = "none")

Figure 2. Bubble Plot of Plastic Pollution From Audits Against GDP for 2019 and 2020

Show the code 
final_merged |>
  mutate(region = countrycode::countrycode(country,
            origin = "country.name",
            destination = "continent"),
         total_normalized = total / num_events, 
         gdp_trillions = gdp / 1e6) |>
  filter_out(country == "EMPTY",
           total == 0,
           is.na(total),
           is.na(gdp_trillions),
           gdp_trillions == 0, 
           is.na(region)) |>
  group_by(country, region, year) |>
  summarize(total_plastic = sum(total_normalized, na.rm = T), 
            gdp_trillions = mean(gdp_trillions, na.rm = T), 
            Population = mean(Population, na.rm = T)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_trillions, y = total_plastic, size = Population, 
             color = region)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
    scale_size(range = c(.1, 15), name ="Population") +
  labs(color = "Region",
       x = "GDP (Trillions)", 
       title = "Plastic Pollution Found in Audits vs. GDP for 2019 and 2020",
       subtitle = "Total Plastic per Cleanup Event",
       y = "",
       alt = "Bubble chart showing plastic items found per cleanup event vs GDP in trillions,
       colored by region and sized by population") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 26000),
                  xlim = c(0,25)) + 
  scale_color_discrete(na.translate = FALSE) +
  scale_x_sqrt() +
  facet_wrap(~ year) +
  theme_light() + scale_color_manual(values = c("Africa" = "mediumspringgreen", "Americas" = "orchid4", 
                                "Asia" = "darkorange", "Europe" = "deeppink", 
                                "Oceania" = "royalblue"))

Data Model for Research Question 2

Show the code 
model_df <- final_merged |>
  group_by(country, year) |>
  summarise(
    total_plastic = sum(total, na.rm = TRUE),
    Population_millions = first(na.omit(Population)) / 1000000,
    pop_density = first(na.omit(pop_density)),
    gdp_billions = first(na.omit(gdp)) / 1000000000,
    region = first(na.omit(region)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    region = as.factor(region),
    log_total_plastic = log(total_plastic)
  ) 

fit_6 <- lm(
  log_total_plastic ~ Population_millions + pop_density + gdp_billions + region,
  model_df
)

tbl <- tidy(fit_6, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    Predictor = dplyr::recode(term,
      `(Intercept)` = "Intercept",
      `Population_millions` = "Population (millions)",
      `pop_density` = "Population Density",
      `gdp_billions` = "GDP (billions)"
    ),
    Predictor = sub("^region", "Region: ", Predictor)
  ) |>
  select(
    Predictor,
    Estimate = estimate,
    `Std. Error` = std.error,
    `t value` = statistic,
    `p value` = p.value,
    `95% CI Lower` = conf.low,
    `95% CI Upper` = conf.high
  )

tbl |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Linear Regression Predicting Logged Plastic Collected by Country, Year**"),
    subtitle = "Response: log(total_plastic)"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Predictor = md("**Predictor**"),
    Estimate = md("**Estimate**"),
    `Std. Error` = md("**Std. Error**"),
    `t value` = md("**t value**"),
    `p value` = md("**p value**"),
    `95% CI Lower` = md("**95% CI Lower**"),
    `95% CI Upper` = md("**95% CI Upper**")
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(Estimate, `Std. Error`, `t value`, `95% CI Lower`, `95% CI Upper`),
    decimals = 4
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = `p value`, decimals = 3) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md(
       "The response variable was transformed as log(total_plastic) to meet linear regression assumptions"
    )
  )
Linear Regression Predicting Logged Plastic Collected by Country, Year
Response: log(total_plastic)
Predictor Estimate Std. Error t value p value 95% CI Lower 95% CI Upper
Intercept 5.4932 1.1349 4.8402 0.000 3.2359 7.7505
Population (millions) 0.0015 0.0012 1.2577 0.212 −0.0009 0.0039
Population Density −0.0006 0.0003 −2.0672 0.042 −0.0012 0.0000
GDP (billions) 160.7701 115.7806 1.3886 0.169 −69.5128 391.0530
Region: Eastern Africa 3.0035 1.5196 1.9766 0.051 −0.0188 6.0259
Region: Eastern Asia −0.5729 1.6740 −0.3422 0.733 −3.9024 2.7566
Region: Eastern Europe 0.8096 1.5195 0.5328 0.596 −2.2126 3.8319
Region: Middle Africa 4.2469 2.5316 1.6776 0.097 −0.7883 9.2821
Region: Northern Africa 0.5925 1.9615 0.3020 0.763 −3.3088 4.4937
Region: Northern America −0.8434 1.9687 −0.4284 0.669 −4.7591 3.0722
Region: Northern Europe 0.2497 1.3883 0.1799 0.858 −2.5116 3.0110
Region: Oceania −1.1579 1.9652 −0.5892 0.557 −5.0666 2.7508
Region: South America 2.1694 1.3395 1.6196 0.109 −0.4948 4.8335
Region: South-Eastern Asia 3.7058 1.3444 2.7564 0.007 1.0318 6.3798
Region: Southern Africa 1.5155 1.9607 0.7730 0.442 −2.3842 5.4152
Region: Southern Asia 0.9942 1.4534 0.6840 0.496 −1.8965 3.8848
Region: Southern Europe 1.4958 1.3082 1.1434 0.256 −1.1061 4.0978
Region: Western Africa 3.5947 1.3614 2.6404 0.010 0.8869 6.3025
Region: Western Asia −1.8473 1.4620 −1.2635 0.210 −4.7551 1.0605
Region: Western Europe 1.3120 1.3705 0.9573 0.341 −1.4139 4.0378
The response variable was transformed as log(total_plastic) to meet linear regression assumptions

A linear regression model was fit to examine how demographic, economic, and regional factors relate to differences in plastic collected during cleanup audits across countries and years. The model included population (in millions), population density, GDP (in billions), and regions as predictors of the log transformed plastic counts. The results show that population density is significantly associated with plastic collected (p = 0.042), indicating that more densely populated countries tend to have lower levels of plastic collected, holding the other factors constant. Regional differences were the strongest predictors, with countries in Western Asia showing significantly lower levels compared to the reference region (p = 0.001). Total population (p = 0.212) and GDP (p = 0.169) were not statistically significant, which suggests that the economic size does not explain variation in plastic collected. The results show that geographic location and population distribution are more important than economic indicators in explaining differences in plastic collected.

Conclusion

We examined two questions about plastic pollution using cleanup audit data and additional country datasets. First, the analysis showed that plastic composition varies across countries, although several categories such as PET, PP, and LDPE appear repeatedly among the most common forms of collected waste. Second, the analysis found that differences in plastic collected across countries are more closely associated with regional context and population density than with GDP alone. Taken together, these results suggest that plastic pollution is shaped by both the composition of consumer waste and the demographic and geographic setting in which that waste is collected.

# The Context of our Data

## Original Data Set

This was a data frame with information on plastic pollution. It was originally collected by the Break Free From Plastic (BFFP) group. They are an environmental activist with an interest in reducing the amount of plastic waste in the world. They focus on reducing the use of single use plastics and other actions across the entire life cycle of plastics. They believe that the best way to address plastic pollution comes from prevention over treating the effects of pollution. The data is two years (2019 and 2020) worth of data collected through global audits that BFFP does trying to understand the sources of plastic waste creation. The information provided by the data set is the country, parent company which created the plastic waste, the number of clean up events and volunteers, and the counts of plastic waste. This was unit-less so we are assuming individual plastic pieces collected at the event. There was a grand total count and multiple columns representing the different types of plastics ranging from the thin material of bags to PVC pipe plastic. Each row represents a country and company combination. There are over 10,000 rows of data.

This data was originally found in multiple folders one each for the years 2019 and 2020. Each folder then had a separate csv file for each country. jonthegeek used map_dfr to combine all the csv files into a single data frame. This function also changed the data types. All were turned into doubles as they were numbers except for the country and parent company as these were characters. He then added a year column as the ideas was to eventually combine the two years together. After cleaning up the names with janitor he did the same thing to the 2020 data. This one had some duplicate columns so he used an if_else function to take care of that. Finally the two years were bound together and some names were changed to make it easier to work with and we now have the data set we will be using.

## Country Statistics Data Set

The second dataset was obtained through a country statistics API and provide demographic and economic indicators for the countries represented in the plastics data. In this dataset, the observational unit is the country. Variables retrieved from the API include measures such as GDP, population, population density, employment by sector, and other indicators. The documentation for the API does not clearly identify the exact reference year for every variable, so this analysis uses variables that are relatively stable across the 2019–2020 period. The data is important since it provides demographic and economic context for understanding country differences in plastic collected during cleanup audits.

## Country Environmental Data Set

The third dataset was obtained through the Global Footprint Network API by pulling country level data for 2019 and 2020 from the National Footprint and Biocapacity Accounts. The API returns environmental and development indicators for countries and territories, including metrics such as Total Biocapacity and Total Ecological Footprint. After retrieving the data, I combined the yearly responses, reshaped them into a country year format, and matched them to the plastics dataset using country and year. This allowed me to incorporate environmental context into the original dataset and compare plastic collection outcomes with a nations ecological state.

# Research Questions

Plastic pollution is not only an environmental problem, but also a social and economic one. Different countries consume, regulate, and dispose of plastic in different ways, so cleanup audits can reveal both what kinds of plastics are most common and how broader characteristics of countries may shape pollution patterns. Given this, we wanted to pose two research questions that guide our analysis first.

#### Question 1

Which plastic types are the most abundant in the audits, does this change from country to country?

#### Question 2

How do demographic and economic characteristics of countries along with their geographic characteristics associated with differences in plastic collected during cleanup audits?

# Research Question 1: Plastic Composition Across Countries

## Tables

To address the first research question, we begin by comparing the composition of plastic waste across countries and years. The following tables summarize the proportions and overall audit profiles for the five most populous countries in the dataset. These summaries help identify which plastic categories are most common and whether those patterns appear consistent across countries.

### Table 1. Proportions of Plastics for the 5 Most Populated Countries

```{r}
#| label: Libraries
#| message: false

library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(httr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
library(ggridges)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(viridis)
library(ggtext)
library(broom)
library(scales)
library(car)

plastics <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2021/2021-01-26/plastics.csv')

plastics_clean <- plastics |> 
  mutate(across(empty:pvc, ~replace_na(., 0)),
         total = rowSums(across(c(empty:pvc))),
         .before = num_events)
final_merged <- readRDS("final_merged.rds")
#final_merged <- read_csv("/Users/avery/Desktop/stat541/project-1/final_table.csv")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-Data-Summary-1
#| tbl-cap: "Proportions of Each Plastic Type for the 5 Most Populated Countries"

proportions <- function(df) {
  
  df |>
    select(country:pvc) |>
    map_if(is.numeric, ~.x / df$grand_total) |>
    as.data.frame() 
}

plastics_clean |>
    mutate(year = factor(year)) |>
    group_by(country, year) |>
    summarize(across(empty:total, 
                     ~sum(.x, na.rm = TRUE)),
              .groups = "drop") |>
  proportions() |>
  filter(country %in% c("India",
                        "China",
                        "United States of America",
                        "Indonesia",
                        "Nigeria")) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_percent(columns = empty:pvc) |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()) |>
  tab_caption("Percentage of Each Plastic Type Found in Audits From 
              the 5 Most Populated Countries by Year") |>
  cols_move_to_end(o) |>
  cols_label(country = "Country", 
             year = "Year",
             empty = "Empty",
             hdpe = "HDPE",
             ldpe = "LDPE",
             o = "Other",
             pet = "PET",
             pp = "PP",
             ps = "PS",
             pvc = "PVC")  |>
  data_color(
    method = "numeric",
    palette = "plasma", 
    reverse = FALSE
  )
```

### Table 2. Summary of Audits for the 10 Countries with the Highest Amount of Plastic Collected

```{r}
#| label: tbl-Data-Summary-2
#| tbl-cap: "Summary Statistics of Audits for the 10 Countries with the Highest Amount of Plastic Collected"
country_profile <- function(data, group_var1, group_var2, min_audits = 10) {
  data |>
    filter(!is.na(country), country != "EMPTY") |>
    group_by({{ group_var1 }}, {{ group_var2 }}) |>
    summarise(
      audits = n(),
      total_pieces = sum(grand_total),
      median_pieces = median(grand_total),
      mean_pieces = mean(grand_total),
      max_pieces = max(grand_total),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) |>
    filter(audits >= min_audits) |>
    arrange({{ group_var1 }}, {{ group_var2 }}) |>
    mutate(country = str_to_title(country)) |>
    arrange(desc(total_pieces)) |>
    slice_head(n = 10) |>
    mutate(country = str_remove(country, "_")) |>
    gt() |> 
    tab_header(
      title = md(
        paste0(
          "**Table.** Plastic cleanup profile by country and year for countries 
          with at least ",
           min_audits,
          " audits."
        )
      )
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(audits, total_pieces, median_pieces, mean_pieces, max_pieces),
    decimals = 1,
    use_seps = TRUE
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    country = md("**Country**"),
    year = md("**Year**"),
    audits = md("**Audits**"),
    total_pieces = md("**Total Pieces**"),
    median_pieces = md("**Median Pieces**"),
    mean_pieces = md("**Mean Pieces**"),
    max_pieces = md("**Max Pieces**")
  )
}


country_profile(plastics_clean, country, year, min_audits = 1) 
```

## Visualizations

### Figure 1. Lollipop Plot of Average Plastic Waster per Audit for 2019 and 2020 for the 5 Most Populated Countries
```{r}
#| label: fig-Vizualization-1
#| fig-cap: "Lollipop Plot of Average Plastic Waster per Audit for 2019 and 2020 for the 5 Most Populated Countries"

rate_ify <- function(df, numerator, denominator, name = rate){
  stopifnot(is.data.frame(df))
  df |>
    mutate({{name}} := {{numerator}}/{{denominator}})
}

plastics_clean |> 
  group_by(country, year) |> 
  summarise(total_trash = sum(total),
            num_events = mean(num_events),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  rate_ify(numerator = total_trash,
           denominator = num_events,
           name = avg_event_impact) |> 
  mutate(country = str_to_title(country)) |>
  filter(country %in% c("India",
                        "China",
                        "United States Of America",
                        "Indonesia", 
                        "Nigeria")) |>
  mutate(country = fct_reorder(country, avg_event_impact)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = avg_event_impact, y = country)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = 0, xend = avg_event_impact,
                   y = country, yend = country),
               color = "gray60") +
  geom_point(size = 4, color = "steelblue") +
  facet_wrap(~ year) +
  labs(
    title = "Average Waste Accounted for per Audit",
    subtitle = "Top 5 Most Populated Countries",
    x = "Average Waste per Audit",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(avg_event_impact, 1)), 
          hjust = -0.3, 
          size = 3) +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Research Question 2: Economic and Geographic Patterns in Plastic Collection

## Visualizations

The following visualizations display how plastic waste patterns differ across broader economic and geographic groupings.

### Figure 1. Ridge Plot Showing Plastic Waste Type Distribution by Country GDP per Capita

```{r}
#| label: fig-Vizualization-3
#| fig-cap: "Ridge Plot Showing the Amount of Plastic Waste for Each Type of Plastic"

final_merged |> 
  filter(year == 2019) |> 
  group_by(country) |> 
  summarize(unknown = sum(empty),
            hdpe = sum(hdpe),
            ldpe = sum(ldpe),
            other = sum(o),
            pet = sum(pet),
            pp = sum(pp),
            ps = sum(ps),
            pvc = sum(pvc),
            total = sum(total),
            gdp_per_capita = mean(gdp_per_capita),
            hdi = mean(HDI),
            .groups = "drop") |> 
  mutate(income_group = case_when(
    gdp_per_capita < 1500 ~ "Low Income",
    gdp_per_capita > 12000 ~ "High Income",
    TRUE ~ "Middle Income")) |> 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = unknown:pvc,
    names_to = "plastic_type",
    values_to = "waste") |> 
  filter(waste <= 3500) |> 
  
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = waste,
                       y = plastic_type,
                       fill = income_group)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_viridis(discrete=TRUE) +
  labs(
    title = "Plastic Waste Type Distribution of Countries by GDP per Capita",
    subtitle = "Divided into <span style='color: #6a546f;'>High Income</span>, 
      <span style='color: #b5aa5f;'>Middle Income</span>,
    and <span style='color: #5e8684;'>Low Income</span>",
    x = "Waste Amount",
    y = "",
    alt = "Density ridge plot showing the density of plastic waste audited
    for multiple types of plastic. Colored by GDP per capita category,
    High, Middle, and Low") +
  theme_dark() +
  theme(plot.subtitle =  element_markdown(),
        legend.position = "none") 

```

### Figure 2. Bubble Plot of Plastic Pollution From Audits Against GDP for 2019 and 2020

```{r}
#| label: fig-Vizualization-2
#| fig-cap: "Bubble Plot of Plastic Pollution From Audits Against GDP for 2019 and 2020"
#| warning: false

final_merged |>
  mutate(region = countrycode::countrycode(country,
            origin = "country.name",
            destination = "continent"),
         total_normalized = total / num_events, 
         gdp_trillions = gdp / 1e6) |>
  filter_out(country == "EMPTY",
           total == 0,
           is.na(total),
           is.na(gdp_trillions),
           gdp_trillions == 0, 
           is.na(region)) |>
  group_by(country, region, year) |>
  summarize(total_plastic = sum(total_normalized, na.rm = T), 
            gdp_trillions = mean(gdp_trillions, na.rm = T), 
            Population = mean(Population, na.rm = T)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_trillions, y = total_plastic, size = Population, 
             color = region)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
    scale_size(range = c(.1, 15), name ="Population") +
  labs(color = "Region",
       x = "GDP (Trillions)", 
       title = "Plastic Pollution Found in Audits vs. GDP for 2019 and 2020",
       subtitle = "Total Plastic per Cleanup Event",
       y = "",
       alt = "Bubble chart showing plastic items found per cleanup event vs GDP in trillions,
       colored by region and sized by population") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 26000),
                  xlim = c(0,25)) + 
  scale_color_discrete(na.translate = FALSE) +
  scale_x_sqrt() +
  facet_wrap(~ year) +
  theme_light() + scale_color_manual(values = c("Africa" = "mediumspringgreen", "Americas" = "orchid4", 
                                "Asia" = "darkorange", "Europe" = "deeppink", 
                                "Oceania" = "royalblue"))
```

# Data Model for Research Question 2

```{r}
#| label: Data Model
#| include: false

model_df <- final_merged |>
  group_by(country, year) |>
  summarise(
    total_plastic = sum(total, na.rm = TRUE),
    Population = first(na.omit(Population)),
    pop_density = first(na.omit(pop_density)),
    employment_industry = first(na.omit(employment_industry)),
    HDI = first(na.omit(HDI)),
    gdp = first(na.omit(gdp)),
    imports = first(na.omit(imports)),
    infant_mortality = first(na.omit(infant_mortality)),
    region = first(na.omit(region)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    region = as.factor(region),
    log_total_plastic = log(total_plastic)
  ) 

fit_1 <- lm(
  log_total_plastic ~ Population + pop_density + region,
  model_df
)

fit_2 <- lm(
  log_total_plastic ~ Population + pop_density + employment_industry + region,
  model_df
)

fit_3 <- lm(
  log_total_plastic ~ Population + pop_density + HDI + region,
  model_df
)

fit_4 <- lm(
  log_total_plastic ~ Population + pop_density + imports + region,
  model_df
)

fit_5 <- lm(
  log_total_plastic ~ Population + pop_density + infant_mortality + region,
  model_df
)


fit_6 <- lm(
  log_total_plastic ~ Population + pop_density + region + gdp,
  model_df
)

fit_7 <- lm(
  log_total_plastic ~ Population + pop_density + region + gdp + imports,
  model_df
)

adj_rsq <- list(
  summary(fit_1)$adj.r.squared,
  summary(fit_2)$adj.r.squared,
  summary(fit_3)$adj.r.squared,
  summary(fit_4)$adj.r.squared,
  summary(fit_5)$adj.r.squared,
  summary(fit_6)$adj.r.squared,
  summary(fit_7)$adj.r.squared
)

adj_rsq


vif_results <- list(
  vif(fit_1),
  vif(fit_2),
  vif(fit_3),
  vif(fit_4),
  vif(fit_5),
  vif(fit_6),
  vif(fit_7)
)

vif_results

```

```{r}
#| label: Data Model Output
#| warning: false

model_df <- final_merged |>
  group_by(country, year) |>
  summarise(
    total_plastic = sum(total, na.rm = TRUE),
    Population_millions = first(na.omit(Population)) / 1000000,
    pop_density = first(na.omit(pop_density)),
    gdp_billions = first(na.omit(gdp)) / 1000000000,
    region = first(na.omit(region)),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    region = as.factor(region),
    log_total_plastic = log(total_plastic)
  ) 

fit_6 <- lm(
  log_total_plastic ~ Population_millions + pop_density + gdp_billions + region,
  model_df
)

tbl <- tidy(fit_6, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  mutate(
    Predictor = dplyr::recode(term,
      `(Intercept)` = "Intercept",
      `Population_millions` = "Population (millions)",
      `pop_density` = "Population Density",
      `gdp_billions` = "GDP (billions)"
    ),
    Predictor = sub("^region", "Region: ", Predictor)
  ) |>
  select(
    Predictor,
    Estimate = estimate,
    `Std. Error` = std.error,
    `t value` = statistic,
    `p value` = p.value,
    `95% CI Lower` = conf.low,
    `95% CI Upper` = conf.high
  )

tbl |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Linear Regression Predicting Logged Plastic Collected by Country, Year**"),
    subtitle = "Response: log(total_plastic)"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Predictor = md("**Predictor**"),
    Estimate = md("**Estimate**"),
    `Std. Error` = md("**Std. Error**"),
    `t value` = md("**t value**"),
    `p value` = md("**p value**"),
    `95% CI Lower` = md("**95% CI Lower**"),
    `95% CI Upper` = md("**95% CI Upper**")
  ) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(Estimate, `Std. Error`, `t value`, `95% CI Lower`, `95% CI Upper`),
    decimals = 4
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = `p value`, decimals = 3) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md(
       "The response variable was transformed as log(total_plastic) to meet linear regression assumptions"
    )
  )
```

A linear regression model was fit to examine how demographic, economic, and regional factors relate to differences in plastic collected during cleanup audits across countries and years. The model included population (in millions), population density, GDP (in billions), and regions as predictors of the log transformed plastic counts. The results show that population density is significantly associated with plastic collected (p = 0.042), indicating that more densely populated countries tend to have lower levels of plastic collected, holding the other factors constant. Regional differences were the strongest predictors, with countries in Western Asia showing significantly lower levels compared to the reference region (p = 0.001). Total population (p = 0.212) and GDP (p = 0.169) were not statistically significant, which suggests that the economic size does not explain variation in plastic collected. The results show that geographic location and population distribution are more important than economic indicators in explaining differences in plastic collected.

## Conclusion

We examined two questions about plastic pollution using cleanup audit data and additional country datasets. First, the analysis showed that plastic composition varies across countries, although several categories such as PET, PP, and LDPE appear repeatedly among the most common forms of collected waste. Second, the analysis found that differences in plastic collected across countries are more closely associated with regional context and population density than with GDP alone. Taken together, these results suggest that plastic pollution is shaped by both the composition of consumer waste and the demographic and geographic setting in which that waste is collected.
